title: Vista Teleportation Analysis: Spatial Cognition in Virtual Environments
text: This report presents the analysis of spatial cognition in virtual environments using the Vista Teleportation paradigm. The study examines how different movement modes (with and without optic flow) and environmental configurations (vista vs. non-vista spaces, small vs. large environments) affect spatial navigation performance.

title: Vista Teleportation Analysis: Spatial Cognition in Virtual Environments
text:
Training Trial Performance
Before analyzing the main experimental trials, we validated participant performance on training trials where they pointed to the origin door. This serves as a basic competency check to ensure participants understood the pointing task.





Distribution of pointing errors during training (origin door) vs. experimental trials. Left panels show training trials, right panels show experimental trials.




Participants demonstrated good accuracy on training trials, with relatively small errors in both distance and angular pointing, confirming they understood the task requirements.

title: Vista Teleportation Analysis: Spatial Cognition in Virtual Environments
text:
Pointing Distance Error
We first examined how accurately participants estimated distances to target objects. The distance error represents the difference between the actual distance to the target and the distance to the location they indicated.





Distribution of pointing distance errors showing potential outliers









Pointing distance errors by experimental conditions. Positive values indicate overshooting, negative values indicate undershooting.





Statistical Model for Distance Error
We fitted a linear mixed-effects model to examine the effects of optic flow, environment size, and vista condition on pointing distance errors. The model included the actual distance to the target as a covariate to account for the fact that larger distances might lead to larger errors. Extreme outliers (|z| > 3) were excluded from the analysis.
Regular mixed effect model with normal distrubution has been used, as the distribution of distance errors was approximately normal around 0-mean.



Linear mixed-effects model results for pointing distance error

The model revealed that vista spaces significantly reduced distance estimation errors compared to non-vista spaces (\(\beta\) = -73.09m, p < 0.05). The presence of optic flow had significant main effects on distance error (model comparison between model with and withouth Optic flow: \(\chi^2\) = 5.01, p = 0.171).

Key Finding: Participants made smaller distance estimation errors in vista spaces (environments with open sightlines) compared to non-vista spaces, regardless of movement mode or environment size. This suggests that visual access to the environment improves distance estimation accuracy.

title: Vista Teleportation Analysis: Spatial Cognition in Virtual Environments
text:
Pointing Angle Error
Next, we examined the angular accuracy of participants’ pointing responses. Angle errors were computed as the absolute difference between the correct direction and the direction indicated by participants.





Distribution of absolute angle errors (log-transformed). The distribution shows positive skew, justifying the use of a Gamma regression model.





Statistical Model for Angle Error
Due to the skewed distribution of the absolute angle errors, we used a generalized linear mixed-effects model with a Gamma distribution (as the log of the errors are left skewed) and log link. To ensure model stability, we set explicit treatment contrasts for all factors.



Generalized linear mixed-effects model results for pointing angle error (Gamma family, log link)

Key Finding: Angle errors were significantly affected by environment size, with larger environments leading to larger angular errors. However, neither movement mode nor vista condition significantly affected angular accuracy. This suggests that angular pointing is more sensitive to spatial scale than to visual access or movement information.

title: Vista Teleportation Analysis: Spatial Cognition in Virtual Environments

Key Finding: Movement mode (optic flow vs. teleportation) did not significantly affect the total distance traveled. However, participants traveled significantly shorter distances in vista spaces compared to non-vista spaces, suggesting more efficient navigation when environmental layout is visible.

Key Finding: While movement method showed some relationship with navigation duration in simple models, this effect was not significant when interaction terms were included in the model. Environmental characteristics (vista condition and size) were more important determinants of navigation time.
